questions.question: With which DI lifetime does `AddHostedService<T>()` register the worker type `T`?
options: Singleton — one instance for the whole process
Scoped — a new instance per request scope
Transient — a new instance each time it is resolved
Scoped at startup, then promoted to singleton after `StartAsync`
questions.explanation: `AddHostedService<T>()` registers `T` as a singleton, so the host creates exactly one instance for the app's lifetime. That is precisely why injecting a scoped `DbContext` into the constructor is a captive-dependency bug.

questions.question: When you derive from `BackgroundService`, which single method do you override to host a long-running processing loop?
options: `ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)`
`StartAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken)`
`RunAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken)`
`OnTickAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)`
questions.explanation: `BackgroundService` implements `IHostedService` for you and exposes the single overridable method `ExecuteAsync(stoppingToken)`, which is where the long-running loop lives. `StartAsync` is already implemented by the base class and `RunAsync`/`OnTickAsync` are not members of `BackgroundService`.

questions.question: What is the default value of `HostOptions.ShutdownTimeout` that bounds how long the host waits for all hosted services to stop?
options: 30 seconds
5 seconds
15 seconds
Infinite — the host waits forever for workers to drain
questions.explanation: `HostOptions.ShutdownTimeout` defaults to 30 seconds; past that, the host stops waiting and proceeds with disposal, potentially cutting off in-flight work. A 15-second value is an example of an explicit override, not the default.

questions.question: Since .NET 6, the default behavior when an unhandled exception escapes `BackgroundService.ExecuteAsync` is to log it and stop the entire host.
questions.explanation: The default is `BackgroundServiceExceptionBehavior.StopHost`: the exception is logged and the whole host (and every other worker) is torn down. Before .NET 6 it was silently swallowed, leaving a zombie process with a dead worker.

questions.question: What is the idiomatic way for a consumer to drain a `System.Threading.Channels` channel until completion?
options: `await foreach (var item in reader.ReadAllAsync(ct))`
`while (reader.TryRead(out var item)) { }` polled on a `Thread.Sleep` loop
`foreach (var item in reader.ToEnumerable())`
`reader.WaitToReadAsync(ct).Result` checked in a tight spin loop
questions.explanation: `await foreach (var item in reader.ReadAllAsync(ct))` is the async-native drain idiom; it yields items as they arrive and ends gracefully once `Writer.Complete()` is called and the buffer empties. The polling/`Thread.Sleep`/spin variants defeat the channel's `await`-able design and waste CPU.

questions.question: Why must a hosted service's `StartAsync` be fast and non-blocking?
options: The host awaits each service's `StartAsync` sequentially before the app is considered started, so slow work there stalls startup
`StartAsync` runs on the UI thread, and blocking it freezes the application window
The host calls `StartAsync` with a 1-second token that cancels any longer work
`StartAsync` runs inside the request pipeline, so blocking it delays every incoming HTTP request
questions.explanation: The host awaits each hosted service's `StartAsync` in registration order before reporting the app as started, so blocking work there delays the whole host coming up. There is no UI thread, no automatic 1-second cancellation, and `StartAsync` runs during host startup, not per-request.

questions.question: A singleton worker injects `IServiceScopeFactory` but creates one scope at the top of `ExecuteAsync` and reuses it for every loop iteration. What is wrong with this?
options: A scope held for the worker's lifetime is the captive bug again — the resolved `DbContext` lives for the whole process and its change tracker grows unbounded
`IServiceScopeFactory` can only create one scope per process, so the second iteration throws
Scopes created outside a request cannot resolve scoped services, so `GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>()` returns null
Nothing is wrong — a single long-lived scope is the recommended pattern for workers
questions.explanation: Creating the scope once and holding it for the worker's lifetime reintroduces the captive-dependency problem: the same scoped `DbContext` lives for the whole process, its change tracker grows without bound, and it isn't safe to reuse. The fix is one scope per unit of work (per tick/message), mirroring a per-request scope.

questions.question: A `POST` endpoint writes to a `Channel.CreateUnbounded<WorkItem>()` and a single worker reads slowly. Under sustained load that outpaces the consumer, what is the failure mode and the correct fix?
options: Memory grows without limit until the process is killed (OOM); fix with a bounded channel using `BoundedChannelFullMode.Wait` to apply backpressure
`WriteAsync` starts throwing `ChannelClosedException`; fix by calling `Writer.Complete()` less often
The endpoint blocks each request until the worker catches up; fix by switching to an unbounded channel
Items are silently dropped once a hidden 1000-item cap is hit; fix by raising the cap
questions.explanation: An unbounded channel never blocks the writer, so the backlog grows until the process runs out of memory. A bounded channel with `BoundedChannelFullMode.Wait` makes `WriteAsync` await when full, throttling producers (the endpoint slows) instead of leaking memory.

questions.question: Why is `PeriodicTimer` preferred over `System.Threading.Timer` for periodic work inside `ExecuteAsync`?
options: You await the tick and then await the work in one loop, so the next tick can't start until the current iteration finishes — preventing re-entrant overlap
`PeriodicTimer` guarantees the work runs at exactly the configured interval with zero drift, unlike callback timers
`PeriodicTimer` automatically retries the work on failure, whereas callback timers do not
`PeriodicTimer` callbacks run on a dedicated high-priority thread, avoiding thread-pool starvation
questions.explanation: Because the tick and the work are both awaited in the same `async Task` loop, the next tick cannot begin until the current iteration returns, eliminating re-entrancy. Callback-based `Timer`s fire on a thread-pool thread regardless of whether the prior callback finished. `PeriodicTimer` does not guarantee zero drift, does not auto-retry, and does not use a dedicated thread.

questions.question: Graceful shutdown is requested while a worker is sitting in `await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5))` with **no** `CancellationToken` passed to `Delay`. What is the consequence?
options: The delay is not cancellable, so the worker keeps sleeping; it only notices shutdown after the 5 minutes elapse, likely exceeding `ShutdownTimeout` and being force-cut
`Task.Delay` throws `OperationCanceledException` immediately because the host cancels all outstanding tasks
The host kills the delay instantly because shutdown overrides any pending await
Shutdown is blocked indefinitely — the host will never proceed until the delay completes
questions.explanation: Cancellation is cooperative: if `stoppingToken` is not flowed into `Task.Delay`, the delay can't observe the shutdown signal and keeps running. The worker only reacts after the delay returns, which can blow past `ShutdownTimeout` and get force-cut. The host does not magically cancel unaware awaits, nor is it blocked forever (it gives up at the timeout).

questions.question: An unhandled exception escapes `ExecuteAsync` while `HostOptions.BackgroundServiceExceptionBehavior` is set to `Ignore`. What is the outcome, and why is `Ignore` usually the wrong choice?
options: The exception is logged, the host keeps running, but that worker stays dead — a silently degraded app, usually worse than crashing and restarting
The exception is swallowed and `ExecuteAsync` is automatically restarted from the top, so the worker recovers transparently
The host stops anyway because `Ignore` only suppresses the log entry, not the shutdown
The worker is replaced with a fresh singleton instance and continues processing
questions.explanation: `Ignore` logs the exception and keeps the host alive, but the faulted worker does not restart — it stays dead while the rest of the app runs on, a silently degraded state. The framework does not auto-restart `ExecuteAsync` or recreate the worker; the recommended pattern is a try/catch inside the loop so the worker survives transient faults.

questions.subtopic: When to use a durable platform
questions.question: Which of the following requirements indicate you should move work OUT of an in-process `BackgroundService` + in-memory channel and onto a durable/distributed platform? (Select all that apply.)
options: The work must survive a crash, redeploy, or pod eviction without being lost
Many instances must drain one shared queue as competing consumers across replicas
A job must run 'every night at 2am, exactly once, even if the box was down'
A low-latency, best-effort in-process handoff where losing a few items on restart is acceptable
Decoupling a fast producer from a single in-process consumer within one process
questions.explanation: Durability across restarts, competing consumers across replicas, and persistent exactly-once scheduling all exceed what an in-process worker can provide and call for a broker, Hangfire/Quartz, or Azure Functions. Best-effort low-latency handoff and single-process producer/consumer decoupling are exactly what in-process `BackgroundService` + channels are for.

questions.question: Which statements about `IHostedService` versus `BackgroundService` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `IHostedService` is the raw `StartAsync`/`StopAsync` contract; `BackgroundService` is the base class implementing it for you
Raw `IHostedService` is well suited to one-shot start/stop hooks like warming a cache or registering with discovery
`BackgroundService` only blocks startup up to the first `await` reached inside `ExecuteAsync`
`BackgroundService.ExecuteAsync` is awaited to completion before the host is considered started
`IHostedService.StartAsync` is fire-and-forget and never awaited by the host
questions.explanation: `IHostedService` is the raw contract (good for one-shot hooks); `BackgroundService` implements it and its `StartAsync` only awaits `ExecuteAsync` up to its first incomplete await, so the loop keeps running in the background. The host does NOT await `ExecuteAsync` to completion, and it DOES await `StartAsync` (it is not fire-and-forget).

questions.question: Given this worker, does the host finish starting promptly?
options: Yes — `ExecuteAsync` reaches its first `await` (`Task.Delay`) on the first iteration and yields, completing `StartAsync` and letting the host start
No — because `ExecuteAsync` is a `while` loop it never returns, so `StartAsync` never completes and the host hangs on startup
No — the synchronous `DoSyncWork()` makes `ExecuteAsync` complete synchronously, blocking startup until the loop ends
Yes — but only because the host always runs `ExecuteAsync` on a background thread regardless of awaits
questions.explanation: `BackgroundService.StartAsync` awaits `ExecuteAsync` only up to its first incomplete await. The first iteration runs `DoSyncWork()` then hits `await Task.Delay(1000, …)`, which yields, so `StartAsync` completes and the host starts while the loop continues in the background. A long synchronous block reachable BEFORE any await would delay startup, but here the work per iteration is short and a `Task.Delay` await is reached on iteration one.

questions.question: In a `BackgroundService` loop, why is the filtered catch `catch (OperationCanceledException) when (stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)` written instead of a bare `catch (OperationCanceledException)`?
options: To swallow only the cancellation that comes from a graceful host shutdown, while letting an OCE from any OTHER source surface as a genuine failure
Because a bare `catch (OperationCanceledException)` does not compile inside an `async` method
Because the `when` filter is required for the catch to actually catch `OperationCanceledException` at all
To convert the `OperationCanceledException` into a successful return so the host treats shutdown as a crash
questions.explanation: The `when` filter ensures you only swallow cancellation caused by the host's `stoppingToken` (a graceful stop); an `OperationCanceledException` originating from some other cancellation source is allowed to propagate as a real error rather than being masked. A bare catch compiles fine, the filter isn't required to catch the type, and the goal is to avoid treating shutdown as a crash, not to cause one.

questions.question: With `AppDbContext` registered scoped and this worker registered via `AddHostedService<Reaper>()`, what happens at startup in a Development host?
options: `Build()` throws — a singleton hosted service cannot consume the scoped `AppDbContext`, so scope validation reports the captive-dependency error
It runs fine because EF Core automatically promotes `AppDbContext` to singleton inside hosted services
It runs but the first database call throws `ObjectDisposedException` at runtime instead of at startup
`ExecuteAsync` is skipped entirely and the worker is silently removed from the host
questions.explanation: In Development, scope validation is on by default, so building the host throws 'Cannot consume scoped service AppDbContext from singleton Reaper' at `Build()`. EF Core does not promote the context's lifetime; the fix is to inject `IServiceScopeFactory` and resolve the context inside a per-iteration scope.

questions.question: A `PeriodicTimer(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10))` ticks on a fixed cadence relative to start, so it measures the interval from one tick to the next regardless of work duration, whereas putting `await Task.Delay(interval)` at the END of the loop measures the interval only AFTER the work finishes.
questions.explanation: `PeriodicTimer` fires at a fixed rate relative to its start (with long-running work causing ticks to be coalesced rather than backlogged), so the cadence is tick-to-tick. Trailing `Task.Delay` instead starts the clock only after each iteration's work completes, giving a fixed gap BETWEEN runs — genuinely different scheduling semantics.

questions.question: How will a captured `BackgroundService` exception be surfaced differently in .NET 11 compared to the .NET 6+ default?
options: `IHost.RunAsync`/`StopAsync` (and `WaitForShutdownAsync`) will throw the captured exception instead of completing silently, making it observable to the code that ran the host
The default will revert to .NET 5 behavior, silently swallowing the exception again
The worker's `ExecuteAsync` will be automatically retried up to three times before the host stops
The exception will be suppressed entirely and only emitted as an OpenTelemetry metric
questions.explanation: In .NET 11, `IHost.RunAsync`/`StopAsync` and `WaitForShutdownAsync` will rethrow the captured `BackgroundService` exception rather than completing silently, so the failure is observable to the host-running code, not just the logs. It does not revert to swallowing, auto-retry `ExecuteAsync`, or downgrade the failure to a metric.
